'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。
賢所御神楽はその発祥は古く[[神代]]に遡るとも言われている{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90}}。形としては小祭だが、[[天岩戸|天の岩戸]]の伝承に由来するとも言われるもっとも古い祭祀の一つ{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。 [[天照大神|天照大御神]]が天の岩戸に御隠れになった時に大勢の神様が歌い踊った神話に因み、[[冬至]]の時期に向けて御神前で[[神楽]]が奏される{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。 [[神饌|ご神饌]]も大祭なみに差し上げられるという{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。
== 歴史 == 神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては宇多天皇によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。 [[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 ==
* [[天皇]] * [[皇室]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[神道]] * [[神楽]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Citation|和書|author=中澤信弘|date=2018-5-5|title=宮中祭祀|publisher=展転社|ref={{SfnRef|中澤信弘|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=大原康男|date=1994-10-20|title=天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度|publisher=展転社|ref={{SfnRef|大原康男|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|dte=2021-12-22|title=なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=中本真人|dte=2016-7-27|title=宮廷の御神楽|publisher=新典社|ref={{SfnRef|中本真人|2016}} }}
{{Normdaten}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:かしこところみかくら}} [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:皇室の儀式]]

'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。

'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。
賢所御神楽はその発祥は古く[[神代]]に遡るとも言われている{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90}}。形としては小祭だが、[[天岩戸|天の岩戸]]の伝承に由来するとも言われるもっとも古い祭祀の一つ{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。 [[天照大神|天照大御神]]が天の岩戸に御隠れになった時に大勢の神様が歌い踊った神話に因み、[[冬至]]の時期に向けて御神前で[[神楽]]が奏される{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。 [[神饌|ご神饌]]も大祭なみに差し上げられるという{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。
== 歴史 == 神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては[[宇多天皇]]によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。 [[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 ==

'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。

'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。
賢所御神楽はその発祥は古く[[神代]]に遡るとも言われている{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90}}。形としては小祭だが、[[天岩戸|天の岩戸]]の伝承に由来するとも言われるもっとも古い祭祀の一つ{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。 [[天照大神|天照大御神]]が天の岩戸に御隠れになった時に大勢の神様が歌い踊った神話に因み、[[冬至]]の時期に向けて御神前で[[神楽]]が奏される{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。 [[神饌|ご神饌]]も大祭なみに差し上げられるという{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。 == 歴史 == [[ファイル:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|岩戸神楽ノ起顕（三代豊国）]]
神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては[[宇多天皇]]によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。 [[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 == {{皇室祭祀}} * [[天皇]] * [[皇室]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[賢所]] * [[八咫鏡]] * [[神道]] * [[神楽]]

revision.comment: 王朝文化の神楽は男性のみによって行われていた事実を追記
revision.text:
'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（近代以前/'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。
[[ファイル:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|人長舞]]

== 歴史 == [[ファイル:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|岩戸神楽ノ起顕（三代豊国）]]
神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。
古代の神楽は女性によって行われていたが、王朝文化における神楽は男性のみによって行われるようになっていた{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては[[宇多天皇]]によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 == {{皇室祭祀}} * [[天皇]] * [[皇室]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[賢所]] * [[八咫鏡]] * [[神道]] * [[神楽]]

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.

The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.

The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]

The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
