'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

== Bibliography == * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakasawa Nobuhiro|date=2018-5-5|title=Imperial Court Ceremonies (宮中祭祀)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Ohara Yasuo|date=1994-10-20|title=The Emperor: Evolution of Theories and the Imperial Household System (天皇-その論の変遷と皇室制度)|publisher=Tenten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Ohara Yasuo|1994}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2021-12-22|title=Why Kagura Survived the Ōnin War (なぜ神楽は応仁の乱を乗り越えられたのか)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2021}} }} * {{Citation|和書|author=Nakamoto Makoto|date=2016-7-27|title=Court Rituals and Imperial Gagaku (宮廷の御神楽)|publisher=Shinten-sha|ref={{SfnRef|Nakamoto Makoto|2016}} }}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.

While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

revision.text:
'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.

It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]

While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

'''Kashikodokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽), previously known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽), is a {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} held annually around mid-December, where {{ill|Mikagura|en|神楽}} is performed at the {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} (formerly the Naishidokoro){{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}.
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jincho-mai dance]]
The origin of Kashikodokoro Mikagura is said to date back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|en|神代}} {{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90}}. While it is a minor festival in form, it is regarded as one of the oldest rituals, linked to the legend of {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|en|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}. It commemorates the myth where a multitude of deities sang and danced when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大神}} hid herself in the Heavenly Rock Cave. The ritual is performed around the winter solstice with Mikagura performed before the deity{{Sfn|Nakasawa Nobuhiro|2018|p=90-91}}. Offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|神饌}}) are presented in a manner similar to major festivals{{Sfn|Ohara Yasuo|1994|p=132}}.
== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.

== History == [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
The origins of Mikagura are said to be in the Japanese myth recorded in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|古事記}}, where the dance and song performed before the Heavenly Rock Cave ({{ill|Ama-no-Iwaya|en|天の岩屋}}) encouraged {{ill|Amaterasu|en|天照大御神}} to emerge{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66-67}}. The Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|古語拾遺}} from the Nara period mentions that {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|en|猨女君}} performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=66}}, and the {{ill|Sanuki no Suke Nikki|en|讃岐典侍日記}} of the Heian period refers to the Mikagura in a manner that connects it to the myth of the Heavenly Rock Cave{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}.
Initially performed by women, Mikagura transitioned to being performed exclusively by men during the rise of imperial culture{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=68}}. The {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|日本三代実録}} records a Mikagura performance for {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|光孝天皇}} in 885{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=76-79}}. The first Mikagura as part of imperial ceremonies is believed to have been at the Kamo Temporary Festival initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|宇多天皇}}{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=82-83}}.
As the Naishidokoro Mikagura, it began during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|一条天皇}} in 1005, following a fire that damaged the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}}). The ceremony was held to console the deity’s will{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}. By imperial command of {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|後朱雀天皇}} in 1038, it became an annual December ceremony{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2016|p=101}}.
During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|南北朝時代 (日本)}}, the Northern Court held Mikagura despite lacking the Three Sacred Treasures, using a "ceremony as if they were present"{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=71-72}}. Meanwhile, in the Southern Court, {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|後村上天皇}} himself performed the Mikagura{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=73}}.
The {{ill|Ōnin War|en|応仁の乱}} disrupted court ceremonies. The palace was occupied, and all court rituals ceased. By 1472, a new Naishidokoro was built at the {{ill|Muromachi residence|en|室町殿}}, and Mikagura was resumed despite challenges{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=120-129}}. The restored Mikagura was attended by court nobles and commoners alike{{Sfn|Nakamoto Makoto|2021|p=129}}.
== Related topics == * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|天皇}} * {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|皇室}} * {{ill|Imperial Court Ceremony|en|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Kashikodokoro|en|賢所}} * {{ill|Yata no Kagami|en|八咫鏡}} * {{ill|Shinto|en|神道}} * {{ill|Kagura|en|神楽}}

14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:賢所御神楽 up to rev 101851922
revision.text:
'''賢所御神楽'''（かしこどころみかぐら）（近代以前/'''内侍所御神楽''' ないしどころのみかぐら）は毎年十二月の中頃に[[賢所]]（旧内侍所）に対して[[神楽|御神楽]]を奏する[[宮中祭祀]]{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。
[[ファイル:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|人長舞]]
賢所御神楽はその発祥は古く[[神代]]に遡るとも言われている{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90}}。形としては小祭だが、[[天岩戸|天の岩戸]]の伝承に由来するとも言われるもっとも古い祭祀の一つ{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。 [[天照大神|天照大御神]]が天の岩戸に御隠れになった時に大勢の神様が歌い踊った神話に因み、[[冬至]]の時期に向けて御神前で[[神楽]]が奏される{{Sfn|中澤信弘|2018|p=90-91}}。 [[神饌|ご神饌]]も大祭なみに差し上げられるという{{Sfn|大原康男|1994|p=132}}。
== 歴史 == [[ファイル:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|岩戸神楽ノ起顕（三代豊国）]]
神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。
古代の神楽は女性によって行われていたが、王朝文化における神楽は男性のみによって行われるようになっていた{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては[[宇多天皇]]によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 == {{皇室祭祀}} * [[天皇]] * [[皇室]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[賢所]] * [[八咫鏡]] * [[神道]] * [[神楽]]

神楽の起源は『[[古事記]]』に記される日本神話の[[天の岩屋]]に[[天照大御神]]が籠った岩屋の前で踊った歌舞にあると言われる{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66-67}}。 奈良時代の神道書『[[古語拾遺]]』にも「[[猨女君|猨女の君]]が神楽を担った」という記述があり{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=66}}、平安時代の『[[讃岐典侍日記]]』の清暑堂御神楽の記述にも「天照大御神が岩戸にお籠りになられなくなったのももっともなこととおもわれる」と御神楽の起源を天の岩屋神話に求めていた記述がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。
古代の神楽は女性によって行われていたが、王朝文化における神楽は男性のみによって行われるようになっていた{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=68}}。 『[[日本三代実録]]』仁和元年（885）十月二十三日条にも[[光孝天皇]]の前で催された神楽の記録がある{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=76-79}}。 朝廷の年中行事としては[[宇多天皇]]によって始められた賀茂臨時祭の還立の御神楽が最初に成立したと考えられている{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=82-83}}。 内侍所御神楽としては[[一条天皇]]の代に寛弘二年（1005）内侍所が火災に遭う出来事があり、神鏡が損傷した。その神慮を慰めるために行われたのが内侍所御神楽の始まりという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。その後、内侍所御神楽は不定期に行われていたが、長歴二年（1038）、[[後朱雀天皇]]の勅命により毎年十二月の年中行事として行われるようになったという{{Sfn|中本真人|2016|p=101}}。
[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝動乱]]期には北朝は三種の神器を持たずに三代が践祚したが、神鏡不在のままに神鏡が存在するかのように内侍所を祀る「如在の礼」という形式をとり内侍所神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=71-72}}。 南朝では神鏡を前にして[[後村上天皇]]自らが本拍子をとり内侍所神楽が行われたという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=73}}。 応仁の乱が発生すると天皇と院は[[室町殿]]に避難し、内裏は[[土岐成頼]]に、仙洞御所は[[畠山義就]]に占拠され陣を敷かれたために全ての朝儀が停止せざるを得なくなった{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=34}}。 文明二年（1470）十二月七日と文明四年（1472）正月二十四日の晩に内侍所が鳴動するという出来事があり、これを神の怒りと感じた朝廷は避難先の室町殿の南庭の南東の角に内侍所を新造した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52}}。文明四年二月二十二日に遷座の儀と御神楽が行われた{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=53-55}}。文明五年の十二月にも内侍所御神楽は準備され[[土御門有宣]]によって日にちも卜定されたが、幕府からの費用が届かず延期された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=120-121}}。文明六年正月二十五日には[[後土御門天皇]]の強い意向により内侍所神楽は行われ、天皇自身も参拝した{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=125-129}}。西軍統治下にいた地下歌人の[[多忠英]]、[[多久時]]を呼び寄せるなど多くの困難を乗り越えて開催された{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=130}}。この時の神楽は公家だけでなく下級官人を含む多くの男女が見学したという{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=129}}。
== 関連項目 == {{皇室祭祀}} * [[天皇]] * [[皇室]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[賢所]] * [[八咫鏡]] * [[神道]] * [[神楽]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:賢所御神楽]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q131912992]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q131912992]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽) (historically known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽) before the modern era) is a [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Miyanaka ritual]] in which a [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performance is dedicated to the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} (formerly the Naishidokoro) annually in mid-December.{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90-91}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jinchō-mai dance]]

Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura is said to have ancient origins, possibly dating back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|ja|神代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Age of the Gods|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90}} While formally classified as a minor festival, it is considered one of the oldest Shinto rituals, possibly deriving from the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]] myth.{{Sfn|Yasuo Ohara|1994|p=132}} 

According to legend, when [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] hid inside the Heavenly Rock Cave, the assembled deities sang and danced to coax her out. Based on this tradition, a kagura performance is held before the divine presence of the Kashiko-dokoro around the time of the [[:en:Winter solstice|Winter Solstice]].{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90-91}} The ritual offerings ([[:en:Shinsen|Shinsen]]) presented during this ceremony are on par with those of grand festivals.{{Sfn|Yasuo Ohara|1994|p=132}}

== History ==
[[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|''The Origins of Iwato Kagura'' (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]

The origins of kagura are traced to the performance of sacred dances before the rock cave where {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} secluded herself, as described in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]].{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=66-67}} 

A reference in the Nara-period Shinto text [[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]] states that the role of kagura was performed by the {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|ja|猨女君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 猿女君|1=Sarume clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572091}} clan.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=66}} The Heian-period ''{{ill|Sanuki Tenji Nikki|ja|讃岐典侍日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Tenji Nikki|qq=|1=The Diary of Sanuki no Suke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632545}}'' describes a ''Seisho-dō Mikagura'' performance in which it was remarked that "it is only natural that Amaterasu no longer remains hidden in the rock cave," reinforcing the connection between kagura and the Iwato myth.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=68}}

Originally, kagura was performed by women, but over time, it became exclusively performed by men within the royal court.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=68}} 

Records from the first year of {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninna era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (885) in the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] document a kagura performance held before [[:en:Emperor Kōkō|Emperor Kōkō]].{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=76-79}} 

The first kagura established as an annual imperial court ritual was the return procession of the {{ill|Kamo Festival|ja|賀茂祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 葵祭|1=Aoi Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773085}} initiated by [[:en:Emperor Uda|Emperor Uda]].{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=82-83}}

The tradition of the Naishidokoro Mikagura began in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], following a fire in the Naishidokoro in Kankō 2 (1005), which damaged the sacred mirror. The performance was conducted to appease the divine will.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}} Initially performed irregularly, in Chōryaku 2 (1038), [[:en:Emperor Go-Suzaku|Emperor Go-Suzaku]] decreed that it should be held annually in December.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}}

During the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], three successive Northern Court emperors ascended the throne without the Three Sacred Treasures. The kagura was performed in a "ceremony as if the mirror were present" (如在の礼).{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=71-72}} In the Southern Court, however, Emperor Go-Murakami personally led the performance before the actual sacred mirror.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=73}}

Following the outbreak of the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], the emperor and retired emperor evacuated to {{ill|Muromachi Palace|ja|室町殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Palace|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 足利将軍家|1=Ashikaga shogun family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28692536}}, while the Imperial Palace was occupied by [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] and the Sento Imperial Palace by [[:en:Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Hatakeyama Yoshinari]], leading to the suspension of all court ceremonies.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=34}} 

In Bunmei 2 (1470) and Bunmei 4 (1472), mysterious rumblings were heard from the Naishidokoro, interpreted as divine anger. In response, the imperial court constructed a new Naishidokoro at the southeast corner of the Muromachi Palace.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=52}} A relocation ceremony and kagura performance were conducted on the 22nd day of the second month of Bunmei 4.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=53-55}}

Although preparations for the Naishidokoro Mikagura were made in December of Bunmei 5, a lack of financial support from the shogunate delayed the ceremony.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=120-121}} However, in Bunmei 6 (1474), under strong insistence from [[:en:Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado]], the performance was held, with the emperor personally attending.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=125-129}} The court summoned underground poets {{ill|Tachūei|ja|多忠英|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachūei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Takuji|ja|多久時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takuji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, overcoming significant challenges.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=130}} It is recorded that the kagura performance was attended by nobles as well as lower-Ranking officials and a large audience of men and women.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=129}}

* [[:en:Yata no Kagami|Yata no Kagami]]

* [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]

== History ==
[[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|''The Origins of Iwato Kagura'' (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]
