[[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|320px|''The Origins of Iwato Kagura'' (by Utagawa Toyokuni III)]]

The origins of kagura are traced to the performance of sacred dances before the rock cave where {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} secluded herself, as described in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=66-67}} 

A reference in the Nara-period Shinto text {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|WD=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}} states that the role of kagura was performed by the {{ill|Sarume no Kimi|ja|猨女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 猿女君|1=Sarume clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572091}} clan.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=66}} The Heian-period ''{{ill|Sanuki Tenji Nikki|ja|讃岐典侍日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Tenji Nikki|1=The Diary of Sanuki no Suke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632545}}'' describes a ''Seisho-dō Mikagura'' performance in which it was remarked that "it is only natural that Amaterasu no longer remains hidden in the rock cave," reinforcing the connection between kagura and the Iwato myth.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=68}}

Originally, kagura was performed by women, but over time, it became exclusively performed by men within the royal court.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=68}} 

Records from the first year of {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninna era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (885) in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} document a kagura performance held before {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|Emperor Kōkō|WD=Q440518|lt=Emperor Kōkō|1=Emperor Kōkō}}.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=76-79}} 

The first kagura established as an annual imperial court ritual was the return procession of the {{ill|Kamo Festival|ja|賀茂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 葵祭|1=Aoi Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q773085}} initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}}.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=82-83}}

The tradition of the Naishidokoro Mikagura began in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}}, following a fire in the Naishidokoro in Kankō 2 (1005), which damaged the sacred mirror. The performance was conducted to appease the divine will.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}} Initially performed irregularly, in Chōryaku 2 (1038), {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|Emperor Go-Suzaku|WD=Q349296|lt=Emperor Go-Suzaku|1=Emperor Go-Suzaku}} decreed that it should be held annually in December.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}}

During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, three successive Northern Court emperors ascended the throne without the Three Sacred Treasures. The kagura was performed in a "ceremony as if the mirror were present" (如在の礼).{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=71-72}} In the Southern Court, however, Emperor Go-Murakami personally led the performance before the actual sacred mirror.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=73}}

Following the outbreak of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, the emperor and retired emperor evacuated to {{ill|Muromachi Palace|ja|室町殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 足利将軍家|1=Ashikaga shogun family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28692536}}, while the Imperial Palace was occupied by {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori|1=Toki Shigeyori}} and the Sento Imperial Palace by {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|en|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|WD=Q11578676|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshinari|1=Hatakeyama Yoshinari}}, leading to the suspension of all court ceremonies.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=34}} 

In Bunmei 2 (1470) and Bunmei 4 (1472), mysterious rumblings were heard from the Naishidokoro, interpreted as divine anger. In response, the imperial court constructed a new Naishidokoro at the southeast corner of the Muromachi Palace.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=52}} A relocation ceremony and kagura performance were conducted on the 22nd day of the second month of Bunmei 4.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=53-55}}

Although preparations for the Naishidokoro Mikagura were made in December of Bunmei 5, a lack of financial support from the shogunate delayed the ceremony.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=120-121}} However, in Bunmei 6 (1474), under strong insistence from {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|WD=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}}, the performance was held, with the emperor personally attending.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=125-129}} The court summoned underground poets {{ill|Tachūei|ja|多忠英|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachūei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Takuji|ja|多久時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, overcoming significant challenges.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=130}} It is recorded that the kagura performance was attended by nobles as well as lower-Ranking officials and a large audience of men and women.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=129}}

'''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura''' (賢所御神楽) (historically known as '''Naishidokoro Mikagura''' (内侍所御神楽) before the modern era) is a {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|WD=Q11453226|lt=Miyanaka ritual|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} in which a {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}} performance is dedicated to the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} (formerly the Naishidokoro) annually in mid-December.{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90-91}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
[[File:Bugak002.jpg|thumb|150px|Jinchō-mai dance]]

Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura is said to have ancient origins, possibly dating back to the {{ill|Age of the Gods|ja|神代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Age of the Gods|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90}} While formally classified as a minor festival, it is considered one of the oldest Shinto rituals, possibly deriving from the {{ill|Amano-Iwato|en|Amano-Iwato|WD=Q3231835|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave|1=Amano-Iwato}} myth.{{Sfn|Yasuo Ohara|1994|p=132}} 

According to legend, when {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} hid inside the Heavenly Rock Cave, the assembled deities sang and danced to coax her out. Based on this tradition, a kagura performance is held before the divine presence of the Kashiko-dokoro around the time of the {{ill|Winter solstice|en|Winter solstice|WD=Q207932|lt=Winter Solstice|1=Winter solstice}}.{{Sfn|Nakahiro Nakazawa|2018|p=90-91}} The ritual offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|Shinsen|WD=Q11591045|lt=Shinsen|1=Shinsen}}) presented during this ceremony are on par with those of grand festivals.{{Sfn|Yasuo Ohara|1994|p=132}}

The first kagura established as an annual imperial court ritual was the return procession of the {{ill|Kamo Festival|ja|賀茂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 葵祭|1=Aoi Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q773085}} initiated by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|qid=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}}.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=82-83}}

The tradition of the Naishidokoro Mikagura began in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|qid=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}}, following a fire in the Naishidokoro in Kankō 2 (1005), which damaged the sacred mirror. The performance was conducted to appease the divine will.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}} Initially performed irregularly, in Chōryaku 2 (1038), {{ill|Emperor Go-Suzaku|en|Emperor Go-Suzaku|qid=Q349296|lt=Emperor Go-Suzaku|1=Emperor Go-Suzaku}} decreed that it should be held annually in December.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2016|p=101}}

During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, three successive Northern Court emperors ascended the throne without the Three Sacred Treasures. The kagura was performed in a "ceremony as if the mirror were present" (如在の礼).{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=71-72}} In the Southern Court, however, Emperor Go-Murakami personally led the performance before the actual sacred mirror.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=73}}

Following the outbreak of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, the emperor and retired emperor evacuated to {{ill|Muromachi Palace|ja|室町殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Palace|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 足利将軍家|1=Ashikaga shogun family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28692536}}, while the Imperial Palace was occupied by {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|qid=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori|1=Toki Shigeyori}} and the Sento Imperial Palace by {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|en|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|qid=Q11578676|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshinari|1=Hatakeyama Yoshinari}}, leading to the suspension of all court ceremonies.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=34}} 

In Bunmei 2 (1470) and Bunmei 4 (1472), mysterious rumblings were heard from the Naishidokoro, interpreted as divine anger. In response, the imperial court constructed a new Naishidokoro at the southeast corner of the Muromachi Palace.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=52}} A relocation ceremony and kagura performance were conducted on the 22nd day of the second month of Bunmei 4.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=53-55}}

Although preparations for the Naishidokoro Mikagura were made in December of Bunmei 5, a lack of financial support from the shogunate delayed the ceremony.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=120-121}} However, in Bunmei 6 (1474), under strong insistence from {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|qid=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}}, the performance was held, with the emperor personally attending.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=125-129}} The court summoned underground poets {{ill|Tachūei|ja|多忠英|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachūei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Takuji|ja|多久時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, overcoming significant challenges.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=130}} It is recorded that the kagura performance was attended by nobles as well as lower-Ranking officials and a large audience of men and women.{{Sfn|Makoto Nakamoto|2021|p=129}}
